title: The Sixth Sunday of Easter
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## May 17, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Sixth Sunday of Easter falls in the season of Easter, occurring 42 days after Easter Sunday (which was April 12, 2020). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date typically include Acts 17:22-31 (Paul's address at the Areopagus), Psalm 66:8-20, 1 Peter 3:13-22, and John 14:15-21 (Jesus's promise of the Holy Spirit). The liturgical color is white, and the themes center on witness, the indwelling of the Holy Spirit, and preparation for Pentecost (occurring 10 days later on May 31).

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church remained in the early stages of reopening after pandemic closures, with the Diocese of Colorado having issued guidance on May 8, 2020 allowing limited in-person worship with strict protocols (masks, distancing, no congregational singing). Bishop Robert Wright of the Diocese of Colorado was navigating rapid changes in pastoral guidance as COVID-19 death rates were still rising nationally. No major General Convention news or bishop elections were occurring at this time, though dioceses nationwide were grappling with the long-term implications of the pandemic on parish life and giving.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the early stages of Colorado's "Safer at Home" phase (Phase 11), which had begun May 1, allowing limited retail and outdoor activities while maintaining restrictions on gatherings and indoor dining. Mayor Michael Hancock's office was managing complaints about enforcement inconsistency and economic hardship on small businesses. Locally, attention remained on the opioid crisis and homelessness, with the pandemic creating additional shelter and social service challenges in the Capitol Hill neighborhood where the Cathedral is located.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The COVID-19 pandemic remained the dominant national story, with the US approaching 1.5 million confirmed cases and 90,000 deaths by mid-May; protests against lockdowns were occurring in various states. The unemployment crisis was deepening, with May jobless claims reflecting the economic devastation of spring 2020. The racial justice movement was beginning to build momentum following the Minneapolis police killing of George Floyd on May 25 (though this would occur 8 days after this sermon, it was not yet a national flashpoint on May 17).

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 pandemic was devastating Brazil, which was beginning to emerge as a global hotspot with thousands of deaths by mid-May despite President Bolsonaro's downplaying of severity. India's COVID case count was rising sharply as the virus spread rapidly through dense urban centers and migrant labor populations. The UK was facing criticism over its handling of the pandemic, with Prime Minister Boris Johnson's government under scrutiny for care home deaths and testing delays.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of profound uncertainty, grief, and exhaustion after two months of lockdown; Americans were experiencing decision fatigue over masks, reopening, and safety protocols. The cultural conversation was fractured between those advocating for continued caution and those demanding rapid reopening, with partisan divisions hardening around pandemic response. Sports remained largely suspended (though limited discussions of NBA and other league restarts were underway), and entertainment consumption had shifted heavily to streaming services; church communities like Saint John's were wrestling with how to be spiritually present to their members while maintaining safety, creating a unique tension between faith and fear.
